name: Why Your Deep Research Agent Fails? On Hallucination Evaluation in Full Research Trajectory (DeepHalluBench)
venue: arXiv preprint (no peer-reviewed venue listed yet at time of search)
authors: Yuhao Zhan, Tianyu Fan, Linxuan Huang, Zirui Guo, Chao Huang [uncertain on first-author institution; corresponding author appears to be Chao Huang]
dataset_size: 100 distinctively hallucination-prone tasks (queries) including adversarial scenarios, spanning 11 distinct domains. Each task generates a multi-step research trajectory when run by a Deep Research Agent (DRA), so the effective number of evaluable units (atomic actions + atomic claims + sub-queries) per task is much larger than 100.
annotation_unit: Multi-granular trajectory decomposition: (1) atomic actions (decomposed plans), (2) atomic claims (decomposed summaries), and (3) sub-queries (search/retrieval steps). Each unit is verified independently against the PIES taxonomy. The headline unit is 'task' (100 of them); the scoring unit is per-trajectory PIES quadrant counts.
ground_truth_source: Hybrid: human-curated hallucination-prone task seeds + LLM-judge evaluation of atomic actions / claims / sub-queries against the taxonomy. The PIES verifier itself appears to be LLM-based but applied to fine-grained atomic units rather than whole responses [uncertain on exact judge model].
judge_model: LLM-based PIES verifier — the paper decomposes plans into atomic actions and summaries into atomic claims and verifies each via an LLM-judge. Exact judge model identity not surfaced in available abstracts [uncertain — likely GPT-4 / Claude / Gemini class].
domain: 11 distinct domains spanning humanities (Art, Music & Literature, History) through technical fields. Designed specifically for Deep Research Agent (DRA) evaluation — i.e., LLM-based systems that iteratively plan, search, and reason to retrieve and synthesize information into a final report.
context_length_range: Variable — emerges from the DRA's iterative search/retrieval rather than a fixed prompt length. Final reports are long-form; total trajectory token budget is large but not bounded by a single context window. Not directly comparable to a fixed 8K/32K cap [uncertain on reported max-trajectory length].
error_taxonomy: PIES Taxonomy — two-dimensional, four-quadrant: dim1 = functional component (Planning vs. Summarization); dim2 = error property (Explicit vs. Implicit). Quadrants: (1) Explicit Planning — deviated/redundant plans; (2) Implicit Planning — neglecting user restrictions; (3) Explicit Summarization — fabricated content / mis-quoted citations; (4) Implicit Summarization — neglecting essential retrieved information. Explicit = wrong content present; Implicit = required content absent.
dynamic_test_generation: false — fixed set of 100 curated tasks. However, the agent's trajectory itself is dynamically generated at evaluation time, so the surface form of any given evaluation run is non-static even though the seed task is fixed.
contamination_risk: low — brand new (Jan 2026), only 100 tasks, designed adversarially to be hallucination-prone. Tasks themselves are unlikely to be in pre-training data of models trained before 2026; the dynamic-trajectory nature further reduces leakage exposure.
license: [uncertain] — not surfaced in available search snippets. GitHub repo at https://github.com/yuhao-zhan/DeepHalluBench would carry the canonical license.
public_data_url: https://github.com/yuhao-zhan/DeepHalluBench (code + data per the paper). Direct HuggingFace mirror not confirmed [uncertain].
annotation_cost_per_sample: Atomic decomposition + per-unit LLM-judge verification across multi-step trajectories — significantly more expensive per task than a single-shot QA benchmark. Expected $5–$20 per task end-to-end at 2026 API rates given trajectory length [uncertain].
reported_metric: Hallucination score H per PIES quadrant + overall H. Lower H = fewer hallucinations. Aggregate H is reported per agent system (OpenAI Deep Research, Gemini Deep Research, Salesforce, Qwen, etc.).
reported_value: Qwen has lowest overall H ≈ 0.149; OpenAI ≈ 0.155; mid-tier Gemini ≈ 0.175 and Salesforce ≈ 0.185. Lower-is-better. (Reported in the paper's main results table.)
overlapping_benchmark: None of our existing benchmarks (MATH-500, GSM8K, GPQA, HotpotQA, TriviaQA, WebQ) overlap. DeepHalluBench is in a fundamentally different regime (multi-step agentic trajectories) from any of our prior phases.
model_used_in_paper: Five proprietary DRAs + one open-source DRA: OpenAI Deep Research, Gemini Deep Research, Salesforce DRA, Qwen DRA, plus others [uncertain on exact lineup of all six].
applicability: medium-to-high for thesis scope, but LOW for a near-term spectral-fusion pilot. DeepHalluBench is the first trajectory-level hallucination benchmark for agentic systems and aligns with Direction 6/7 (agentic trajectory) of our roadmap. However, it requires running a full Deep Research Agent stack (iterative planning + tool use + retrieval), which is far outside our current single-model H(n)-extraction pipeline. Replication would require building a DRA harness first.
preemption_risk: medium — DeepHalluBench partially OVERLAPS with our agentic trajectory direction by introducing PIES (a categorical taxonomy of trajectory hallucinations). However: (a) they use LLM-judge categorical labels, NOT entropy / spectral / frequency-domain features; (b) they do not access white-box model internals (most DRAs are proprietary); (c) their detection unit is the atomic action/claim verified against external sources, not the entropy trajectory of a single rollout. Our spectral H(n) framing is structurally orthogonal: we model the model's own uncertainty trajectory, not external claim-level verifiability. The PIES taxonomy is a useful complementary framework we should CITE when positioning our work in agentic settings, but it does not pre-empt the spectral contribution.
bracha_alignment: high — PIES quadrant counts and H scores are exactly the kind of categorical / continuous noisy estimates that LTT, COIN, conformal calibration, and PPI are designed to risk-control. Bracha's PPI is especially natural given that the PIES verdicts themselves come from LLM-judges (noisy labels).
ofir_alignment: moderate — full research trajectories are richer time series than single-rollout entropy traces; multi-view kernel / VSDE methods could in principle map a (plan, retrieve, summarize, plan, ...) trajectory into a state-space embedding. However, getting the full trajectory's intermediate states from proprietary DRAs is a hard access problem.
notes: STRATEGIC: cite PIES as the first taxonomy of agentic hallucinations and position our spectral H(n) work as the FIRST WHITE-BOX trajectory-aware detector — distinct from PIES which is black-box and judge-based. Practical caveat: replicating DeepHalluBench end-to-end requires either (a) using only the open-source Qwen DRA where we can extract token logits, or (b) waiting for the field to release open-weight DRA stacks. For a 1-day pilot this is NOT a starting benchmark — start with FACTS Grounding or L-CiteEval and only graduate to DeepHalluBench once a DRA harness is built. arXiv ID 2601.22984 is consistent with a Jan 2026 submission and the paper is fresh enough that contamination risk is genuinely low.
model_size_range: Proprietary closed DRAs (OpenAI, Gemini, Salesforce) + Qwen DRA (open source). Qwen DRA exposes weights and would be in-distribution for our white-box pipeline; the others are black-box only.
gpu_requirements: Running a DRA requires concurrent inference + tool calling + retrieval orchestration. For Qwen DRA at 7B–72B class on A100 80GB, single-GPU is feasible for the LLM call but the full agent harness (search, browsing, retrieval) is the dominant engineering cost, not GPU.
sample_size_for_replication: 100 tasks in the full benchmark. Subsetting is possible but the H-score variance with fewer tasks is unclear [uncertain].
min_samples_for_replication: ~30–50 tasks for stable per-quadrant PIES estimates [uncertain — paper reports on full 100].
runtime_estimate: Per task: a full DRA trajectory (planning + multiple search/retrieval rounds + summarization) — minutes to hours per task in wall-clock time, dominated by retrieval round-trips and final-report synthesis [uncertain on exact per-task runtime].
